coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots coupled bioelectrocatalysis for enhanced methane productivity in anaerobic co-digestion of sewage sludge and food waste: Focusing on enhancement mechanisms and microbial community succession
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Lei
Zhang, Shuting
Heng, Shiliang
Wang, Jiandong
Xia, Mengting
Tian, Jiahua
Wang, Jiayi
Su, Jinghan
Lu, Xueqin
Zhen, Guangyin
coredata.dc:description: The limited electron transfer efficiency and poor stability of microorganisms are challenges in traditional bioelectrocatalytic food waste treatment. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) possess excellent biocompatibility and electrochemical properties. When combined with bioelectrocatalysis, CDs can regulate microbial community structure and enhance electron exchange capacity. The results demonstrated that compared with the control group (28.1 mL/g-VS/d), the CDs at a dosage of 0.50 g/g VS could increase CH4 production by a factor of 7.8. CDs not only increase CH4 production but also improve the digestate’s stability, making it suitable for use as bio-fertilizer. Moreover, a significantly high Methanobacterium richness (11.6 %) signified an intensified utilization of hydrogen and formic acid pathways in CH4 production. Particularly, the biocompatible CDs could be absorbed by microorganisms, forming an environmental network that was more conducive to electron transfer with unabsorbed CDs and accelerating interspecies electron transfer. This work provides mechanistic insights into boosting CH4 production in AD.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-based materials
Resource regeneration
Anaerobic digestion
Digestate stability
Microbial community structure

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: EPS extraction and parallel factor analysis
The effect of CDs on the CH4 production
Morphological changes on electrodes caused by CDs
Effect of carbon dots (CDs) on EPS secretion in anaerobic sludge
Determine the specific components of EPS affected by CDs through EEM-PARAFAC analysis
Community structure of carbon felt on electrodes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was sponsored by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China, the Science & Technology Innovation Action Plan of Shanghai (No. 21230714000), Chongqing Natural Science Foundation (2022NSCQ-MSX2112, and CSTB2023NSCQ-MSX0546), and Shanghai Municipal Bureau of Ecology and Environment (Shanghai Environmental Science No. 40).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Biogas production, NH4 +- N, and VFAs observed at different CDs concentrations during the whole 75 d experiment: (a) daily biogas production rate; (b) accumulated methane production in three stages (point is the actual value, curve is the Gompertz model fitting); the changes of VFAs and NH4 +- N: (c) R1-0.00 g/g VS-0.8 V, (d) R2-0.15 g/g VS-0.8 V, (e) R3-0.25 g/g VS-0.8 V, (f) R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8 V, (g) R5-1.00 g/g VS-0.8 V and (h) R6-control.

After 75 d of experimentation, electrochemical tests were conducted in situ on reactors under different CDs concentrations: (a) CV curves of cathode; (b) EIS curves of cathode (the insets indicate the magnification at high frequency region and the equivalent circuit); (c) ETSA was tested in stage Ⅰ and Ⅱ.

After EPS was extracted, protein and polysaccharide contents were measured. 3D-EEM and PARAFAC analysis were employed to identify the components exerting the greatest influence on microbial EPS after the addition of CDs: (a) PN and (b) PS content of the S-EPS, LB-EPS and TB-EPS, and the ratio of PN/PS; (c-e) Fractional fluorescence EEMs of sludge after three stages (75 d) (deducting Raman scattering and Rayleigh scattering); (f) The intensity of five fluorescence substances in each EPS fraction under different CDs concentrations; Representative EEM and fluorescence spectral loading of the two components (g1 and g2) identified by PARAFAC analysis.
Thermogravimetric analysis was performed to evaluate the thermal stability of digestates: (a) TG, (b) DTG, (c) mass loss, and (d) DSC graphs for the digestates of different CDs concentrations.

Sludge samples of R1-0.00 g/g VS-0.8 V, R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8 V and R5-1.00 g/g VS-0.8 V were selected for microbial community analysis, and the effects of CDs addition on microbial community were explored through the abundance changes at (a) domain, (b) phylum, (c) genus levels; the carbon felts of R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8 V electrodes were selected to observe the microbial enrichment on the anode and cathode surface, and the differences in abundance at (a) domain, (d) phylum and (c) genus levels proved that different reactions occurred on the surface; (e) network analysis based on relative abundance of microbes; (f) properties and enhancement mechanisms of anaerobic co-digestion of SS and FW.
Kinetic characteristics based on modified Gompertz model.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots coupled bioelectrocatalysis for enhanced methane productivity in anaerobic co-digestion of sewage sludge and food waste: Focusing on enhancement mechanisms and microbial community succession

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Lab for Urban Ecological Processes and Eco-Restoration
School of Ecological and Environmental Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The limited electron transfer efficiency and poor stability of microorganisms are challenges in traditional bioelectrocatalytic food waste treatment. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) possess excellent biocompatibility and electrochemical properties. When combined with bioelectrocatalysis, CDs can regulate microbial community structure and enhance electron exchange capacity. The results demonstrated that compared with the control group (28.1 mL/g-VS/d), the CDs at a dosage of 0.50 g/g VS could increase CH4 production by a factor of 7.8. CDs not only increase CH4 production but also improve the digestate’s stability, making it suitable for use as bio-fertilizer. Moreover, a significantly high Methanobacterium richness (11.6 %) signified an intensified utilization of hydrogen and formic acid pathways in CH4 production. Particularly, the biocompatible CDs could be absorbed by microorganisms, forming an environmental network that was more conducive to electron transfer with unabsorbed CDs and accelerating interspecies electron transfer. This work provides mechanistic insights into boosting CH4 production in AD.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Globally, approximately 1.3 billion tons of food are wasted annually and will reach 2.5 billion by 2025 (Huang et al., 2022). Due to its high organic content, food waste (FW) has enormous potential for bioenergy recovery, and its reuse can reduce resource waste. Compared to incineration and composting, anaerobic digestion (AD) offers high efficiency and simple operation advantages in FW treatment, which makes it very promising (Ismail et al., 2022). Meanwhile, co-digestion of FW with other substrates has been proven to improve efficiency (Paranjpe et al., 2023). FW co-digestion with SS improves efficiency, boosts energy, aids environmental protection and waste utilization (Zhang et al., 2023). Additionally, it provides many environmental benefits, such as the ability to generate renewable energy sources such as CH4, and biogas residue generated by AD can replace fertilizers. However, due to the complex composition of substrates, anaerobic co-digestion of FW and SS has drawbacks such as microbial community adaptation difficulties, biogas quality instability, and biogas residue utilization limitations (Liu et al., 2023b). Therefore, in order to fully utilize the resources of FW and maximize its value, it is urgent to find a method for anaerobic co-digestion of FW and SS.

Over the past decades, bioelectrocatalysis has emerged as an AD-efficiency enhancer. Among them, MEC (Microbial electrochemical cells) is one of the most common techniques in microbial electrocatalytic technology, and it is also widely used in AD (MEC-AD) (Long et al., 2023). MEC-AD integrates electrodes and a power source into an anaerobic digester to generate electricity, improving degradation, biogas production and AD stability. Due to its advantages of low cost, non-toxicity, and sustainability, MEC-AD is one of the most effective means to alleviate energy crises and reduce environmental damage. However, the performance of MEC reactor leaves much to be desired for its low electron transfer efficiency and poor stability, which hindered its large-scale application (Meng et al., 2023).

Recently, adding electron-transfer media like transition metals can enhance MEC capabilities. These mediators possess excellent catalytic properties, which can accelerate key reactions. They can also improve the electrical conductivity of electrodes to reduce internal resistance and enhance the activity and adhesion of microorganisms (Lu et al., 2020). Ma et al. found that the presence of Fe(III) can enhance the biological reduction of Cr(VI) in the microbial electrolysis cell with a biological cathode (Ma et al., 2022a). Compared to metal additives, carbon-based materials are more cost-effective, and the enormous variety of carbon-based materials has made them potential AD performance improvers (Gopinath et al., 2020). Song et al. found that the addition of 2.5 g/L pine-cone biochar increased the cumulative CH4 production during the AD of alcoholic wastewater by 71 % compared to the blank group (Song et al., 2024). Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel zero-dimensional carbon nanoluminescent material, are composed of sp2/sp3 carbon core and outer oxygen/nitrogen functional groups. Compared with other carbon-based materials, CDs offer several distinct advantages. They are smaller in size (＜10 nm), facilitating easier dispersion. Exhibiting quantum confinement effects that endow them with unique physical and chemical properties, CDs can readily penetrate cells. Moreover, they feature abundant surface functional groups, high chemical stability, and excellent biocompatibility (Liu et al., 2023c). Moreover, due to the presence of dielectric confinement and edge effects, CDs have unique chemical and electronic properties, which can promote intercellular electrical connections in AD processes, enhance IET and thus improve the performance and stability of AD of FW (Liu et al., 2024a). However, the effects of CDs on the performance of AD bioelectrocatalysis and microorganisms are rarely reported.

In this work, CDs were combined with bioelectrochemistry to explore whether they positively affect the anaerobic co-digestion of FW and SS. CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method using sodium citrate as a precursor. After synthesis, the effect of CDs coupled bioelectrocatalysis on SS and FW anaerobic co-digestion was studied in a modified serum bottle. During the experiment, essential indicators such as biogas yield, CH4 content, VFAs, and ammonia nitrogen were monitored, and the electrochemical characteristics of CDs and anodes, along with the thermal stability of biogas residue and fertilizer efficiency, were measured to evaluate the impact of CDs coupled bioelectrocatalysis. Finally, through microbial community analysis, particularly the abundance changes of hydrolysis bacteria, acid-producing bacteria, and methanogenic archaea, the potential mechanism of CDs promoting organic biotransformation was revealed.

Anhydrous citric acid used for the preparation of CDs was purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sewage sludge was taken from a sewage treatment plant in Shanghai, China. FW was obtained from the canteen of East China Normal University, in Shanghai, China. After removing non-biodegradable materials (e.g., plastic, chopsticks and bones), FW was diluted with tap water and mixed using an electric mixer, and then large particles were pre-screened through a ten-mesh sieve. SS and FW were mixed at the optimal SS/FW ratio of 1: 1 (based on VS), and stored at 4 °C before use. Inoculum was obtained from an existing AnMBR, which has been running stably for over two years in the lab.

Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by the thermal polymerization method (Zhu et al., 2016). In detail, 5.0 g citric acid and 20 mL deionized water were stirred at 500 r min−1 for 1 h to form a homogeneous mixed suspension. Subsequently, the resulting solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and then the autoclave was sealed and placed at 200°C for 3 h. During this process, citric acid underwent a hydrothermal reaction under high temperature and high pressure, thus forming CDs. After the reaction, a clear light-yellow solution was obtained.

Then, after freeze-drying the reaction solution, the resulting solid product was dried overnight in a vacuum and the yellow powder was collected. The product was characterized by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) (see supplementary material). The results confirmed the successful synthesis of CDs. The synthesis rate of CDs by this method could reach 43 %, which was consistent with the previously reported results (Pundi & Chang, 2022).

The modified 500 mL polyethylene bottle reactor was used as a reactor. The bottle cap was replaced with a four-way stainless steel feeding bottle cap, with one hole connected to an air bag for biogas collection, one hole for sampling and adding FW, and the remaining two holes for anode and cathode. The reference electrode was installed into the hole on the side for subsequent measurement of related electrochemical indicators. In the startup stage, the mixture of SS and FW was mixed with inoculum in a VS ratio of 1: 2, with a total VS of 10 g per bottle, the CDs of different concentrations were added and the remaining space was filled to 500 mL by distilled water (see supplementary material). After fully mixing, high-purity nitrogen was injected for 10 min to ensure the anaerobic environment. During the whole experiment, the gas and sludge samples were taken regularly for further analysis.

The CH4 production potential, maximum CH4 production rate, and reaction lag phase of the system were simulated and calculated using the modified Gompertz model:
P =
P 0
exp
{ - exp
[
R max
· e ·
t 0
- t
/
P 0
+ 1 ]
}
where P represents the cumulative CH4 production rate (mL/g-VS); P0 represents the CH4 production potential (mL/g-VS); Rmax represents the maximum CH4 production rate (mL/d/g-VS); t0 represents the lag phase (d); t represents the experimental time (d); and e represents the Euler constant (Pasalari et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: EPS extraction and parallel factor analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) were collected through a thermal extraction program, including soluble EPS (S-EPS), loosely bound EPS (LB-EPS), and tightly bound EPS (TB-EPS) (Tang et al., 2017). The three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (TD-EEM) spectra of EPS were measured by using an F-4700 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Ltd., Japan), with scanning emission (Em) wavelengths and excitation (Ex) wavelengths both ranging from 200-550 nm. The excitation and emission slit widths were maintained at 5 nm increments, and the scanning speed was set at 12000 nm min−1. The EEM dataset was modeled using parallel factor analysis (PARAFAC). PARAFAC was used to estimate the true latent EEM spectrum. It estimates the true latent EEM spectrum by minimizing the sum of squares of the residuals in a trilinear model. This is achieved by computing an alternating least squares algorithm. Then the EEM data set is simplified into a set of trilinear terms and a residual array (Sgroi et al., 2017).

Synchronous thermal analysis, including thermogravimetry (TG), differential thermogravimetry (DTG) analysis and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), was performed using a thermal analysis-mass spectrometry instrument (Mettler Toledo, Switzerland, STA8000-MS). The experiment was carried out in an argon atmosphere with a flow rate of 75 mL/min. The temperature for measurement ranged from 30 to 700 °C, and the heating rates were set at 5 and 10 °C/min.

The sludge and electrodes (carbon felt) samples were collected from each reactor at the end of the experiment and stored in an ultra-low temperature refrigerator at −80 °C for microbiological analysis. Then the V4-V5 hypervariable region of extracted DNA was amplified with universal 16S rRNA gene-specific primer pair, i.e. 515F (5′-GTGCCAGCMGCCGCGG-3′) and 806R (5′-GGACTACNNGGGTATCTAAT-3′), and performed PCR amplification in Bio-Rad (Bio Rad Laboratory, CA, USA). Finally, EZNA gel extraction test kit (Omega, USA) was used to purify the labeled PCR product. And the sequencing procedure was completed on the Majorbio platform. The raw sequencing data have been submitted to the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) with accession number PRJNA1227019.

Biogas composition was detected by gas chromatography (Shimadzu GC-2014C, Japan) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (SHINAGAWAW-NK-0.5). The chemical oxygen demand (COD), ammonia nitrogen (NH4 +- N), total solids (TS), and volatile solids (VS) were measured according to the Standard Methods (APHA, 2005). Volatile fatty acids (VFAs) were measured by using a gas chromatograph equipped with a DB-WAX-ETR column (7890-GC-5975 ms, Agilent, US). Proteins (PN) were measured by the corrected Lowry procedure at 750 nm wavelength using bovine serum albumin as the standard. Polysaccharides (PS) were determined by phenol–sulfuric acid method at 490 nm wavelength with glucose as the standard (Liu et al., 2024b). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were tested using the in-situ three electrode method on an electrochemical workstation (Cakici et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of CDs on the CH4 production
ce:section.ce:para: Under different CDs concentrations, biogas production changed significantly. The daily biogas production and cumulative CH4 production in different reactors are shown in Fig. 1 . In the stage Ⅰ, all the groups with CDs coupled bioelectrocatalysis started up quickly. And there was a consistent trend in biogas releasing rate between CDs concentration and biogas releasing rate (Fig. 1a, b). By comparing R1-0.00 g/g VS-0.8 V and R6-control, we explored the impact of bioelectrocatalysis, which can increase CH4 production by 2.8 times.

As CDs addition varied from 0.15 to 1.00 g/g VS, biogas production and CH4 concentration were correspondingly promoted, indicating that the addition of CDs can improve biogas production. It was obvious that R5-1.00 g/g VS-0.8 V had the best performance, with the CH4 yield of 247.0 mL/g-VS, yet its best performance did not carry over the next two stages. In the next two stages with no extra CDs addition, the performance of R3-0.25 g/g VS-0.8 V (266.8 mL/g-VS) and R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8 V (268.1 mL/g-VS) was better than R5-1.00 g/g VS-0.8 V (200.2 mL/g-VS). Surprisingly, the best performance of R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8 V (269.6 mL/g-VS) maintained until the third stage ended. Based on the kinetic characteristics of modified Gompertz model (Table 1 ) (Santos et al., 2020), Rmax increase from 6.3 to 40.4 mL/g-VS/d with CDs concentration from 0.15 g/g VS to 1.00 g/g VS. Under different CDs dosages, P was significantly improved according to modified Gompertz model fitting results. In the second and third stages, compared to the maximum methane potential (P) without extra CDs, 0.25 g/g VS CDs dosage performed outstandingly, reaching 120.7 mL/g-VS/d and 90.1 mL/g-VS/d, respectively. According to the model simulation results, the ability of enhancing CH4 production capacity lied mainly in λ and improving Rmax. The promotion of AD was not positively correlated with CDs concentration, and the promotion effect of the highest concentration was not better than that of lower concentrations. Apparently, the model fitting results indicated that when the CDs concentration was added at 0.50 g/g VS, CH4 conversion rate for AD of FW was the optimal.

The changes in NH4 +- N and VFAs are displayed in Fig. 1c-h. During the first three days of reactor startup, the VFAs concentration in the control group was lower than that in the blank group, possibly due to the promotion of VFAs utilization by bioelectrocatalysis. Moreover, from 0.00 to 1.00 g/g VS, the concentration of VFAs increased with the increase of CDs concentration. It could be inferred that the addition of CDs could promote the generation of VFAs, indicating that the organic matter hydrolysis was accelerated. And among various VFAs, the proportion of acetic acid was the highest, which was conducive to the CH4 production process, because only formic acid and acetic acid in VFAs could be directly utilized by methane bacteria (Chen et al., 2024). Subsequently, the methanogenesis process was much easier and rapidly proceed meanwhile VFAs had no accumulation. Generally speaking, NH4 +- N concentration below 200 mg/L is beneficial for anaerobic processes (Guo et al., 2023).

In general, too high NH4 +- N concentration has an adverse effect in AD processes (Yellezuome et al., 2022), while Fig. 1c-h exhibited that CDs coupled bioelectrocatalysis could control NH4 +-N concentration within the suitable range for AD. By combining the description of actual CH4 production among the control group and the experimental groups, it could be known that CDs coupled bioelectrocatalysis may enhance acidogenesis and methanogenesis production steps in the four steps of AD and reduce the accumulation of NH4 +- N.

CDs coupled bioelectrocatalysis increased CH4 production efficiency by 780 %. For simplicity in preliminary economic evaluation, this study focused on direct operational costs such as CDs synthesis (citric acid, hydrothermal process, stirring electricity) and voltage application to assess the feasibility of large-scale CH4 production from FW and SS. The equipment costs (carbon felt, titanium mesh, and reactors) were not included due to their reusability across multiple experiments. CH4 revenue was calculated by pure CH4 price, potentially overestimating actual biogas value because of different CH4 concentrations in treatments (see supplementary material). From the final net value results, it was observed that the net income was the highest when the CDs dosage was 0.50 g/g VS.

CDs coupled bioelectrocatalysis, significantly enhancing CH4 production shows promising potential for increasing biogas revenue, although actual economic benefits would depend on factors such as equipment lifespan, biogas quality, and scale-up conditions. This may potentially stimulate the profitability of the anaerobic co-digestion project of FW and SS. Beyond the revenue derived from CH4, digestates underwent further treatment. The biogas residue from the experiment can serve as bio-fertilizer, and the environmental value it yields is immeasurable.

CV curve is used to reveal the redox capacity of the biofilm on the electrode. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the CVs of the cathode with proper CDs addition show a larger redox current than R6-control. CV normalizing results show that CDs addition can significantly enhance electron transfer and speed up the reaction rate (Wang et al., 2020). For the cathode, R1-0.00 g/g VS-0.8 V had no redox peaks, while R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8V had an indistinct redox peak at around −0.4 V, possibly from the electroactive substance secreted by microbes. The CV curve's integral area represents specific capacitance. When the CDs concentration was 0.50 g/g VS, the specific capacitance reached its maximum value of 0.8 F/cm2, which indicated stronger electronic storage capacity. For the anode (see supplementary material), the addition of CDs remarkably enhanced the area of cyclic voltammetry spectroscopy. These differences represented better electron transfer exchange capability and could also make it easier for bacteria with electron conductivity to adhere to the anode surface.

In this work, EIS characterized cathode electrode electron transfer (Fig. 2b, c). The EIS curve's farthest X-axis projection from the origin represents total impedance, and the first X-axis intersection represents Ohmic resistance (Wang et al., 2021). The semicircle diameter (Rct) of experimental groups (1.3–4.0 Ω) was smaller than that of R1-0.00 g/g VS-0.8V (8.4 Ω), indicating CDs promote electron exchange rate. To further verify the interaction between the cathode anaerobic digestion biofilm and electrode interface, Nyquist curves were fitted with equivalent circuits to calculate the electron transfer resistance of each part (Fig. 2b, c) (Anantharaj et al., 2021). The R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8V cathode had the minimum total polarization internal resistance (Rt = Rohm + Rfilm + Rct, only 5.0 Ω), far less than that of R1-0.00 g/g VS-0.8V (23.6 Ω). Similarly, in the high-frequency region of the anode EIS (see supplementary material), the addition of CDs reduced the diameter of the anode's semicircle, indicating an improvement in the anode's electron transfer exchange capabilities and catalytic activity. This evidence validates that CDs can promote and augment the electrocatalytic performance of AD biofilms.

The electron transfer system activity (ETSA) can reflect intracellular charge generation ability (Fig. 2d). At stage Ⅰ, the ETSA in groups with different dosages of CDs (53.8–80.6 μg·mg−1·h−1) was significantly higher than that without CDs (12.5 μg·mg−1·h−1), which indicated that the addition of CDs would generate more charges. In the subsequent two stages, due to the lack of continuous addition of CDs, ETSA slightly decreased. However, compared to R1-0.00 g/g VS-0.8 V, the impact of the addition of CDs on microorganisms remained higher in terms of sustainability. This indicates that CD addition's positive effect on cell charge generation capacity was sustainable. Therefore, CDs coupled with bioelectrocatalysis significantly promoted electron transfer (i.e., DIET) within the system, enhanced the electron transfer exchange ability, and reduced the internal resistance value of the system, thereby further promoting the degradation of SS and FW to produce CH4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological changes on electrodes caused by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed morphologies and microstructures of the electrode surface (carbon felt) were characterized by SEM (Huang et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2024). The SEM images of R1-0.00 g/g VS-0.8 V and R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8 V electrode after the whole experiment were presented. The electrodes of R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8 V, which had the best CH4 production performance, were mainly composed of rod-shaped microorganisms (see supplementary material). Speculatively, the bacteria attached to the surface of carbon felt were Methanosaeta or Methanobacterium, two common methanogenic archaea. As can be seen from the SEM images, the microbial communities were closely related and tightly stacked together, tightly wrapping the fibers on the surface of the carbon felt, confirming that this phenomenon was caused by EPS secreted by bacterial communities. But in the absence of CDs, bacteria secreted a small amount of EPS, hindering adhesion to the electrode surface. Consequently, adding CDs could enhance microbial growth during anaerobic digestion and promote EPS secretion. As a result, microorganisms could adhere to the surface of the electrode, thus enhancing the electron transfer exchange capabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of carbon dots (CDs) on EPS secretion in anaerobic sludge
ce:section.ce:para: EPS, secreted by microorganisms, mainly consists of PN and PS, and is crucial for sludge-related processes such as cell stability and coagulation (Wen et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, the changes of three types of EPS during the experiment with different dosages of CDs were analyzed. It was found that after the addition of CDs, the total EPS increased by more than twice compared to the group without CDs added. Especially the content of TB-EPS in the reactor of R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8 V increased by nearly three times. Furthermore, a significant change occurred in the PN/PS of EPS. The ratio of PN/PS can affect the respiratory activity of microorganisms and, to some extent, represent the potential for CH4 production, showing a positive correlation (Jin et al., 2022). The PN/PS ratio of R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8 V was 8.9 ± 0.2 and that of R1-0.00 g/g VS-0.8 V was 4.6 ± 0.1, indicating that sludge in a reactor with the CDs concentration of R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8 V had a better promotion of CH4 production.

The EEM fluorescence regional integration (FRI) is commonly used to analyze EPS changes in anaerobic microorganisms before and after CDs addition (Zhao et al., 2024). After 75 days of reaction, two obvious fluorescence peaks were observed (Fig. 3c-e). Through fluorescence indices (FI) in regional integration, it was found that CDs enhanced the fluorescence intensity of dissolved organic matter (DOM) in all layers of EPS, especially the tryptophan-like protein in Region Ⅱ and soluble microbial byproducts (SMBP) in Region Ⅳ.

CDs addition can stimulate EPS, especially TB-EPS secretion, enhance sludge aggregation, and maintain biological-particle stability, beneficial for strict-anaerobic-microorganism enrichment like methanogens and acidogenic bacteria. TB-EPS increase stabilizes sludge flocs. Regular shaking loosens sludge flocs, but CDs-promoted TB-EPS protects them. Consequently, CDs induced microorganisms to secrete more TB-EPS. The TB-EPS adhered to the electrodes, facilitated EET, and expedited the AD process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determine the specific components of EPS affected by CDs through EEM-PARAFAC analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the changes in the secretion of EPS components by microorganisms caused by the addition of CDs, parallel factor analysis (PARAFAC) was conducted. PARAFAC models with 2–10 components were computed, and the correct number of components in the data set was validated by core consistency diagnostics and split-half analysis. In the ideal PARAFAC model, the FMS would be higher than 95 % (Baghoth et al., 2011). In this work, the FMS was over 95 % when the components were divided into two, whereas this FMS declined when divided into three (or four). Hence, the fluorescence peaks were ascertained as a two-component model, and they were validated through split-half analysis. Subsequently, the fluorescence properties were decomposed (Fig. 3g1, g2) according to the loading scores of excitation and emission wavelengths in the applied PARAFAC model. The representative EEM and spectral loading for the two common components were shown Fig. 3. Then, the separated component spectra were examined in the online OpenFluor database and identified as fulvic acid-like and tryptophan-like fluorophores based on Tucker's congruence coefficients. Fulvic acid-like can be beneficial to CH4 production through enzyme binding and electron transfer (Shi et al., 2023). The characterization of EEM-PARAFAC components provided insights into the composition and fate of EPS in the sample, and could be used to gain a deeper understanding of CDs coupled electrocatalysis for AD. It could be observed that the accumulation of fulvic acid-like and tryptophan-like fluorophores in EPS would become a promoting factor for the conversion of substrates into biogas.

Stability is crucial for evaluating digestate as bio-fertilizers. Thus, thermogravimetric analysis (TG) was used to measure the thermal stability of the digestates of all reactors. Fig. 4 a exhibited the TG curves of four digestates during pyrolysis, divided into three stages based on DTG curves (Fig. 4b). In the first stage (50–200 °C), the mass loss is mainly from water evaporation. In the second stage (200–400 °C), the mass loss is mainly due to the pyrolysis of simple organic compounds. In the third stage (400–700 °C), the mass loss is due to the pyrolysis of difficult to degrade organic materials (possibly lignin based materials) (Yun et al., 2018). Fig. 4c shows the mass loss of digestes at each TG curve stage. The total mass losses of R1-0.00 g/g VS-0.8 V, R3-0.25 g/g VS-0.8 V, R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8 V, and R5-1.00 g/g VS-0.8 V were 51.3 %, 49.8 %, 48.2 %, 47.1 % respectively. These values were consistent with organic matter degradation and CH4 production, indicating that the total mass loss of the sample increases as the concentration of CDs rises. This result further indicated that the addition of CDs could achieve better substrate degradation, improve the efficiency of substrate conversion to CH4, and leave less complex organic residues in the biogas residue, related to the CD concentration.

Two exothermic peaks emerged during the digested materials' pyrolysis (Fig. 4d). The first at about 300 °C signaled simple organic matter degradation (carbohydrates, aliphatic structures, hemicellulose), and the second at around 500 °C represented char-formation and lignin decomposition. Apparently, in the DSC curves, the group with CDs added exhibited lower heat flux than the group without CDs added at approximately 300 °C and 500 °C. The results of TG, DTG, and DSC are consistent with those of CH4 yield, which further indicates the positive synergistic effect of CDs in the AD process. The aforementioned thermal stability results show that CDs coupled bioelectrochemistry effectively degrades organic matter with little residue left, demonstrating the potential of digestate as a stable bio-fertilizer component.

In order to further reveal the effect of CDs addition on anaerobic digestion, the microbial composition on the anaerobic sludge and carbon felt was studied. The biodiversity of bioelectrocatalysis solutions under different concentrations of CDs was investigated by 16S rRNA genes. The relative abundance of microorganisms of sludge and electrodes at the domain, phylum and genus level was shown in Fig. 5 .

The abundance of R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8 V archaea was higher than that in R1-0.00 g/g VS-0.8 V and R5-1.00 g/g VS-0.8 V (Fig. 5a). Increased archaea abundance may indicate enhanced CH4 production performance. The top 20 microorganisms with the highest relative abundance at the phylum and genus levels were identified (Fig. 5b and c). The dominant phyla in all groups were Firmicutes, Synergistota, Actinobacteriota and Chloroflexi.

The abundance of Firmicutes in all groups reached over 10 %, and the dosage of 0.50 g/g VS reached 13.1 %, an increase of 23.6 % compared to the non-added group. On the basis of previous studies, Firmicutes is a common dominant bacterial community in anaerobic digestion, as well as the most dominant bacterial community in the hydrolysis and acidification stage, and plays an important role in the degradation of lignocellulose to produce VFAs (Ma et al., 2022b). In this work, the abundance of Firmicutes was the highest, which was consistent with the previous measurement results of VFAs.

As shown in Fig. 5c, the top three bacterial genera with the abundance of R1-0.00 g/g VS-0.8 V were Thermovirga (7.8 %), Methanosaeta (6.8 %), and Georgenia (6.7 %). The dominant bacterial genus of 0.50 g/g VS was Thermovirga (16.1 %), Mesotoga (15.8 %), and Methanobacterium (11.6 %). The dominant bacterial genera of R5-1.00 g/g VS-0.8 V was Advenella (23.6 %), Thermovirga (18.2 %) and Mesotoga (11.2 %). Different CDs concentrations led to slightly different dominant bacterial genera in each group, yet all groups included Thermovirga. Thermovirga were reported to accelerate hydrolysis and generate small molecules. Thermovirga plays a role in DIET during AD (Shi et al., 2022). The increase in the abundance of Thermovirga proved that the addition of CDs could promote the hydrolysis process of anaerobic digestion and the communication between microorganisms. Mesotoga with multiple highly thermally stable hydrolase-encoding genes was assumed to contribute to methanogenesis by utilizing polysaccharides and monosaccharides to promote CH4 production. The addition of CDs significantly increased the abundance of Mesotoga, which was beneficial for biogas production.

The abundance of important phylum-level microorganisms related to CH4 production such as Methanobacterium, Methanosaeta, Methanosarcina, Methanospirillum and Methanofastidiosum, increased sharply after the addition of CDs. In particular, the abundance of CH4 producing bacteria in R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8 V with CDs reached 11.6 %, nearly three times that of R1-0.00 g/g VS-0.8 V (3.0 %). Based on metabolic pathway differences, CH4 production occurs via three known pathways: hydrogenotrophic methanogens (HMs, producing CH4 from CO2 with hydrogen and/or formate as reducing agents), acetoclastic methanogens (AMs, using acetate as the sole substrate), and methylotrophic methanogens (MMs, producing CH4 from the methyl group in organic compounds) (Liu & Whitman, 2008). The addition of CDs did not change the way of CH4 production, which still relied mainly on hydrogenotrophic methanogens. Instead, it enhanced the activity of microorganisms in this process, thus increasing CH4 production.

Under high-concentration CDs stress, the microbial community structure changed. Interestingly, Advenella was detected in R5-1.00 g/g VS-0.8 V. Advenella can degrade macromolecular organic matter, especially phenolic substances, and can adapt to environmental factors. Advenella is strongly negatively correlated with TVFAs (total volatile fatty acids), SCOD (soluble chemical oxygen demand), TN, and pH. The decrease in these indicators under R5-1.00 g/g VS-0.8 V conditions suggested why this bacterium's content was much higher than in the other two groups. It was also reported that the relative content of Advenella increases when AD was inhibited by high concentrations, which was a stress response to environmental inhibition (Li et al., 2022). Overall, this indicated that when the concentration of CDs reaches 1.00 g/g VS, the internal environment did not further promote the growth of anaerobic digestion microorganisms, representing that there was an optimal concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Community structure of carbon felt on electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Microbial analysis of the electrode (carbon felt) (R4-0.50 g/g VS-0.8 V) was conducted. Although the carbon felts of the anode and cathode were in the same environment as the container for AD reaction was a single chamber, different reactions occurred on their surfaces, resulting in different bacterial distributions and structures. Clearly, the abundance of archaea in the cathode was higher than that in the anode. Then, at the phylum level (Fig. 5d), the abundance of Actinobacteriota and Euryarchaeota in the cathode was much higher than that in the anode, indicating an increase in microorganisms that could directly receive electrons from the cathode (Liu et al., 2023a).

In the reactor, anode-attached electrochemically active microorganisms convert organic matter into CO2, electrons, and protons. The electrons moved through a closed circuit to the cathode and are consumed to produce CH4 (Choi & Lee, 2019). The abundance of methanogenesis genus (e.g., Methanobacterium, Methanosaeta, Methanosarcina) at the cathode was 44.6 % higher than at the anode (Fig. 5c). Geobacter was found on the anode with an abundance of 2.8 %, while on the cathode was only 0.1 %. Geobacter, a well-studied long-distance EET-capable microorganism, possesses metallic-like conductivity like organic semiconductors, and can generate OmcZ nanowires with high electron conductivity, promoting DIET (direct interspecies electron transfer) (Gu et al., 2023). Compared to anaerobic sludge and carbon felt without CDs, almost no Geobacter was detected, indicating that the addition of CDs can increase anode Geobacter abundance, accelerate anode electron output, and thereby enhance CH4 yield. Trichococcus was almost non-existent in the anaerobic sludge, but was found on the carbon felt, and its abundance accounts for a considerable proportion, with an abundance of 15.4 % on the anode and 13.7 % on the cathode. More specifically, Trichococcus continuously enriches the carbon felt in the reactor with CDs added. Trichococcus is a common acid producing bacterium that can utilize various organic substances, such as acetate, formate, glucose to produce lactic acid, formic acid, acetic acid, and CO2. Some studies found that Trichococcus plays an important role in the degradation of grease such as glycerol (Vikromvarasiri et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2018). The accumulation of VFAs, specifically propionic acid, is one of the limiting factors for anaerobic digestion of CH4 production. Then, Trichococcus is also an electroactive iron-reducing bacterium and may enhance performance by an electrosyntrophy with Methanbacterium concilii-like methanogens (Ma et al., 2021). From the actual biogas production, it could be inferred that an increase in the abundance of Trichococcus on carbon felt can establish a new electroactive connection with Methanobacterium. This connection suggested that archaea associated with Methanobacterium could directly receive electrons from Trichococcus, promoting rapid electron transfer and accelerating CH4 generation.

In order to elucidate the effect of CDs coupled bioelectrocatalysis on facilitating the anaerobic co-digestion of FW and SS, a possible mechanism is proposed in Fig. 5d and e. The internal effects can be summarized as two primary aspects: (i) The coupling of CDs with bioelectrocatalysis promotes the increase in the abundance of bacteria (Thermovirga, Mesotoga, Methanobacterium etc.) related to anaerobic digestion, thereby enhancing the anaerobic digestion performance. (ii) CDs serve as intermediaries, which boost the efficiency of electron transfer among microorganisms.

CDs coupled bioelectrocatalysis showed great potential to improve the performance of anaerobic co-digestion of SS and FW. The results showed that CDs were anchored on functional microorganisms or in the biofilm layer by self-assembly. Meanwhile, the constructed artificial electron transport chain and bioelectrochemical processes have increased the number of dominant methanogenic archaea by nearly four folds, altered the microbial distribution and strengthened the electron exchange, resulting in a 620 % enhancement in electron transfer activity. At 0.50 g/g VS of CDs, CH4 conversion was highest, reaching 77 % of the theoretical value of COD conversion, and its enhancement effect on AD lasted the longest, making FW digestion more complete and the digestion solution suitable for use as bio-fertilizer. This work showed that CDs coupled bioelectrocatalysis was a feasible strategy for substrate degradation in AD.

Lei Lv: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shuting Zhang: Validation. Shiliang Heng: Investigation. Jiandong Wang: Visualization. Mengting Xia: Methodology. Jiahua Tian: Software. Jiayi Wang: Methodology. Jinghan Su: Visualization. Xueqin Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Guangyin Zhen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was sponsored by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China, the Science & Technology Innovation Action Plan of Shanghai (No. 21230714000), Chongqing Natural Science Foundation (2022NSCQ-MSX2112, and CSTB2023NSCQ-MSX0546), and Shanghai Municipal Bureau of Ecology and Environment (Shanghai Environmental Science No. 40).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2025.132290.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
